content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 25814
Technical Assistance Administration · · · Executive Legal · · · Enforcement · · · Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                    322-5991              322-6641           322-5444

December 22, 1977

77-12-085

John D. Flitner
City Attorney
6750 Commerce Blvd.
Rohnert Park, California 94928

Dear Mr. Flitner:

Thank you for your letter of November 22, concerning the disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act. The question that you have asked us to resolve is whether a Councilman may vote on a project when he is a potential subcontractor, and the contractor has bid or intends to bid on the project. You have asked us to assume that the potential subcontractor and the contractor who will be soliciting bids have a history of doing business together in the past.

Government Code Section 87100_1/ provides that:

No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

Section 87103(a) and (c) states:

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

_1/ All references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

John D. Flitner
December 22, 1977
Page Two


(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made...
[Emphasis added.]

The Councilman has two possible financial interests that could be affected by the decision in question. The first interest is the Councilman's subcontracting business, which is both an investment and a source of income to him. The second interest which could be affected is the contractor who represents a source of income to the Councilman. Assuming that the decision will have a material effect on both financial interests, (See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 enclosed) it still must be determined whether the decision will foreseeably affect such interests for the purposes of disqualification.

The Commission, in an opinion requested by Tom Thorner, 1 FPPC Opinions 198 (No. 75-089, December 4, 1975), addressed the issue of foreseeability in a question similar to the one that you have raised for our consideration. In that opinion, the Commission stated that if the official has no known connection with the project, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the official will be financially affected. However, if the official is preparing or has made a bid with the serious hope of receiving the award, then it is reasonably foreseeable that the official's interest could be materially affected and he must disqualify himself.

In the situation you have described to us, the official is not the contractor for the project but a potential subcontractor. The Commission pointed out in Thorner that if the contractor has been awarded the contract, or it is likely that his bid may be successful, foreseeability is present if the official has regularly done business with the contractor in the past. The Commission also stated that the potential effect is foreseeable even if the contractor has not selected the official as a subcontractor, and there are other potential subcontractors who could be selected other than the official. If the Councilman is a potential subcontractor, and it is likely that the contractor will be awarded the project, then the Councilman must disqualify himself if a material financial effect upon his business could result from the governmental decision.

Although the Commission offers guidelines for determining foreseeability in Thorner, the guidelines are not conclusive. The Commission makes it clear in Thorner that a case-by-case approach should be taken for determining foreseeability. "...[W]hat is reasonably foreseeable must depend on the facts and circumstances of each specific situation." Thorner 1 FPPC Opinions 198, 207.

John D. Filtner
December 22, 1977
Page Three

As mentioned previously, Sections 87100 and 87103(c) state that an
official must disqualify himself from participating in a decision where it
is foreseeable that the decision could have a material financial effect on
a source of income of $250 or more. Section 82030 defines income to include,
"...[A] pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in
which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially,
a 10% interest or greater." Given that the Councilman owns 100% of the
subcontracting business, if he receives payments from the contractor totaling
$250 or more within twelve months prior to the time when the governmental
decision is to be made, the contractor will represent a source of income
to the official. Consequently, so long as the contractor is a source of
income, the Councilman must disqualify himself from participating in any
decision where it is foreseeable that the decision could have a material
financial effect on the contractor distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally.

I hope that this information has been helpful. If you have any questions
concerning this matter, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Dwight Dickerson
Staff Attorney
Conflicts of Interest Division

DD:mfa
Enclosure

CITY OF ROHNERT PARK
6750 COMMERCE BOULEVARD
ROHNERT PARK, SONOMA COUNTY, CALIFORNIA 94928
TELEPHONE 795-5416

November 22, 1977


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Gentlemen:

As you know, Mayor Beary has been accused of being in conflict
of interest by a variety of individuals and groups in the City
of Rohnert Park. The District Attorney has reported on the matter
and has made his finding in a written opinion which issued recently.
As you may know, Mayor Beary is a plastering contractor within the
City of Rohnert Park who does contract work both within and outside
of the corporate limits of the City. Mayor Beary requested that I
contact you to ask the following questions:

1. May a councilman vote on a project wherein he is not the
contractor but is a potential subcontractor on the project but has
not bid thereon? For your information, Mr. Beary is the potential
subcontractor and the party soliciting bids is a person with whom
Mr. Beary has done business in the past.

2. The second question involves the same general circumstances but
the contractor has either bid or intends to bid on the project. I
have reviewed the McPhail opinion with the Rohnert Park City Council
and I believe that opinion answers the questions proposed by Mayor
Beary. For your information, I have felt that a councilman or
mayor may vote on a project if he has no reason to believe that he
will be a bidder on the project in question and has not been a bidder
in the past. I feel that the issue of foreseeability arises when a
councilman has bid projects in the past and either because of the
association with the prime contractor or the fact that the prime
contractor is in the practice of requesting bids from a variety of
bidders, one of whom is usually the public official, then the
relationship is more than casual and it is probably reasonably
foreseeable that the public official bidding the job will or may
occasionally be awarded the job. I assume there is no question he
will be awarded the job if he is the low bidder. How often he will be
awarded the job when he is not the low bidder I am not in a position
to answer or speculate about.

November 22, 1977

In the examples given above, it would seem that the public official
might be able to vote on a project under example one given above
if a prior relationship had not been established between the bidder
solicitor and the public official.

Simply stated, it would seem to me that the party soliciting the
bids, if new to the area and not having requested bids from the
public official before and if the bids were requested after the
approval of the project and assuming that there was no agreement
between the parties, that the ability of the public official to bid
was contingent upon support of the project, no conflict arises.

Whereas, in a situation wherein the solicitor of the bids has been
in the community for sometime, is known to be in that type of
business and is known to solicit bids from a variety of bidders, then
foreseeability is much more probable.

In the second example given, it seems to me that if the public
official intends to bid or has bid but the award has not been made
prior to the time a vote comes on the project, then foreseeability
is present.

Mayor Beary has requested the Fair Political Practices Commission to
respond to the two questions proposed.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
JOHN D. FLITNER
City Attorney

JDF/ies
